past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, diagnosed 4 years ago, infrequent episodes
Iron deficiency anemia, treated and resolved 2 years ago
family_history: Mother with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed at age 42
One younger brother with no significant medical history
No known family history of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases

social_history.occupation: Graphic designer, works primarily on computer
social_history.exercise: Yoga twice weekly; has recently stopped running due to new visual symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Attention intact on serial sevens. Language fluent without paraphasic errors. Immediate recall 3/3, delayed recall 3/3 at five minutes. No cognitive impairment on bedside testing.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light and accommodation. Visual acuity 20/20 right eye, 20/60 left eye with decreased color saturation in the left eye. Left homonymous hemianopia on confrontation visual field testing. No relative afferent pupillary defect identified. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 strength throughout bilaterally. Lower extremities: 5/5 bilaterally. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout all extremities. Vibration sense intact at bilateral great toes. Pinprick intact bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. No dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait pattern. Tandem gait intact without difficulty. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No Lhermitte sign. No meningismus. Patient reports that photophobia has been persistent since symptom onset. She also describes intermittent floaters in the left visual field.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute left-sided vision loss with headache for 5 days
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Lucia Vega is a 29-year-old right-handed Hispanic woman who presents to the emergency department with a five-day history of progressive left-sided visual field loss. She reports that she first noticed difficulty seeing objects to her left while driving home from work five days ago, initially thinking her glasses were smudged. Over the next 24 hours, the visual deficit became more pronounced and she began having difficulty reading text on her computer screen, missing words on the left side of the page. She also developed a bifrontal headache that she describes as a constant dull pressure, rated 5 out of 10 in severity, which is different in character from her typical migraines. The headache has been accompanied by mild photophobia and intermittent floaters in the left eye. She denies any associated nausea, vomiting, or phonophobia. She has no history of similar visual symptoms. She reports that approximately fifteen months ago she experienced a two-week episode of numbness and tingling involving the right side of her face and right arm that resolved spontaneously. She attributed this to work-related stress and did not seek medical evaluation at the time. She denies any recent head trauma, fevers, chills, weight loss, joint pains, oral ulcers, or skin rashes. She denies any recent travel or sick contacts. Her primary care physician referred her to the emergency department after an urgent noncontrast CT scan of the head showed a hypodense area in the right parieto-occipital region with surrounding edema, raising concern for acute ischemia versus a mass lesion. Given her age and the acute presentation, she was admitted for further workup. On review of systems, she endorses mild fatigue over the past several months but denies any limb weakness, difficulty walking, urinary or bowel dysfunction, or swallowing difficulties. Her family history is notable for autoimmune disease in her mother, which has raised concern for a possible autoimmune etiology.

findings.location: Right parieto-occipital white matter extending to the splenium of the corpus callosum

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Incomplete ring enhancement (open ring pattern) with the opening oriented toward the cortex
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion centrally; mild restricted diffusion along the enhancing margin, likely reflecting active demyelination rather than acute infarction

findings.mass_effect: Mild local mass effect with compression of the trigone of the right lateral ventricle; no midline shift
findings.borders: Open ring enhancement pattern with the non-enhancing portion facing the gray matter, highly suggestive of demyelinating etiology

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter adjacent to the anterior horn of the left lateral ventricle
Right periventricular white matter adjacent to the body of the right lateral ventricle

additional_observations: Surrounding vasogenic edema extends into the subcortical white matter of the right occipital lobe
No additional enhancing lesions identified beyond the dominant lesion
Several smaller T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions are present in the periventricular and juxtacortical white matter, raising the possibility of prior subclinical demyelinating events
No hemorrhage or calcification within the dominant lesion
Normal brain parenchymal volume for age
mri.impression: Large (4.2 cm) right parieto-occipital white matter lesion with open ring enhancement, surrounding edema, and mild mass effect. The open ring enhancement pattern strongly favors a demyelinating etiology (tumefactive demyelinating lesion) over neoplasm or infection. Multiple additional smaller T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in periventricular and juxtacortical white matter without enhancement, potentially representing prior subclinical demyelinating activity. The dominant lesion explains the patient's left homonymous hemianopia due to involvement of the right optic radiations. Differential considerations include tumefactive multiple sclerosis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM), CNS lymphoma, or high-grade glioma, though the imaging morphology favors demyelination. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No evidence of atrial enlargement or ventricular hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. No findings to suggest cardiac source of embolism or arrhythmia.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: ESR 22 mm/hr (H), ANA 1:80 speckled  (H), Vitamin D, 25-hydroxy 18 ng/mL (L).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Present — 3 unique bands in CSF not found in paired serum sample
special_tests.Cytology: No malignant cells identified; no atypical lymphocytes
special_tests.Flow cytometry: No monoclonal B-cell population identified; findings inconsistent with CNS lymphoma

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cm. WBC: 8 cells/uL (95% lymphocytes, 5% monocytes) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 52 mg/dL. Glucose: 58 mg/dL (ratio 0.63 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Present — 3 unique bands in CSF not found in paired serum sample. IgG index: 0.78 (elevated; reference: <0.70). IgG synthesis rate: 14.2 mg/day (elevated; reference: <8.0 mg/day). Myelin basic protein: 8.6 ng/mL (elevated; reference: <4.0 ng/mL). Cytology: No malignant cells identified; no atypical lymphocytes. Flow cytometry: No monoclonal B-cell population identified; findings inconsistent with CNS lymphoma. CSF VDRL: Nonreactive.

findings.location: C5-C6 level, lateral spinal cord on the right

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense lesion in the lateral cord

additional_observations: Single short-segment lesion (<1 vertebral segment) in the lateral cord, a location typical of demyelinating disease
Cervical and thoracic disc spaces preserved without significant degenerative changes
output.impression: Short-segment morphology and peripheral location favor MS over NMOSD. Combined with brain MRI findings, the patient now has lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord locations, meeting criteria for dissemination in space across at least three typical MS regions. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.type: MR spectroscopy of the dominant right parieto-occipital lesion

signal_characteristics.Choline_Cr_ratio: Mildly elevated (1.4; normal <1.2), consistent with active demyelination or gliosis
signal_characteristics.NAA_Cr_ratio: Reduced (1.3; normal >1.6), indicating neuronal injury or axonal loss
signal_characteristics.Lactate_peak: Small lactate doublet present, suggesting anaerobic metabolism from active inflammation
signal_characteristics.Lipid_peak: Mild lipid signal consistent with myelin breakdown

additional_observations: High-grade gliomas typically show markedly elevated choline (Cho/Cr >2.0) and very low NAA, which is not seen here
The presence of a lactate peak supports active inflammatory demyelination
output.impression: See findings above. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: tumefactive multiple sclerosis treatment management corticosteroids plasmapheresis

results.title: Tumefactive demyelination: clinical, radiological, and prognostic features
results.authors: Lucchinetti CF, Gavrilova RH, Metz I, et al.
results.key_finding: Tumefactive demyelinating lesions (>2 cm) can mimic neoplasm or abscess. Open ring enhancement pattern is a key differentiator. Most patients respond to high-dose IV corticosteroids. Approximately two-thirds of patients with tumefactive demyelination are ultimately diagnosed with MS.

results.title: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis: a rare presentation requiring differentiation from CNS neoplasm
results.authors: Hardy TA, Reddel SW, Barnett MH, et al.
results.key_finding: IV methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 5 days) is first-line treatment. Plasmapheresis or IVIG may be considered in steroid-refractory cases. Brain biopsy should be reserved for cases where imaging and laboratory findings are inconclusive. Most tumefactive MS patients transition to typical RRMS and require disease-modifying therapy.

results.title: Distinguishing tumefactive demyelination from glioma: role of MR spectroscopy and perfusion imaging
results.authors: Kiriyama T, Kataoka H, Ueno S, et al.
results.key_finding: MR spectroscopy in tumefactive demyelination shows mildly elevated choline and reduced NAA with lactate peak, whereas gliomas show markedly elevated choline (Cho/Cr >2.0). Perfusion imaging shows lower cerebral blood volume in demyelinating lesions compared to gliomas.

output.summary: Tumefactive demyelinating lesions (>2 cm with mass effect and edema) are an uncommon presentation of MS that can mimic brain tumors. Key imaging discriminators include the open ring enhancement pattern and MR spectroscopy features. First-line treatment is high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3-5 days), with plasmapheresis as second-line for steroid-refractory cases. Brain biopsy may be necessary if noninvasive workup is inconclusive. Approximately two-thirds of patients with tumefactive demyelination are eventually diagnosed with relapsing-remitting MS and should be started on disease-modifying therapy after acute treatment.

output.proposed: Methylprednisolone 1000 mg IV daily for 5 days
interactions: Ibuprofen: Concurrent use of corticosteroids and NSAIDs increases risk of GI bleeding and peptic ulceration. Consider adding proton pump inhibitor prophylaxis and discontinuing ibuprofen during steroid course.
Ethinyl estradiol/norgestimate (oral contraceptive): Corticosteroids may reduce contraceptive efficacy. Advise additional barrier contraception during and for one cycle after steroid course.
warnings: Monitor blood glucose during high-dose IV steroid therapy — risk of steroid-induced hyperglycemia
Insomnia and mood changes are common during pulse steroid therapy; consider administering doses in the morning
GI prophylaxis with PPI recommended given concurrent NSAID use
Consider DVT prophylaxis given inpatient status and immobility
output.formulary_status: Standard formulary — first-line for acute MS relapse and tumefactive demyelination
alternatives: Dexamethasone (alternative corticosteroid, sometimes used in initial management when tumor is on the differential)
Plasmapheresis (PLEX) — 5-7 exchanges over 10-14 days — for steroid-refractory cases
IVIG 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days — alternative to PLEX in steroid-refractory cases

findings.type: Previously identified tumefactive lesion — interval decrease in size
findings.size: Decreased from 4.2 cm to 2.1 cm in maximum dimension

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Reduced signal intensity compared to prior
signal_characteristics.T1: Residual T1 hypointensity centrally (evolving black hole)
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: No enhancement (complete resolution of prior ring enhancement)

findings.mass_effect: Resolved; no compression of lateral ventricle

additional_observations: Complete resolution of enhancement confirms treatment response
Development of T1 hypointensity (black hole) at the lesion center suggests some permanent tissue injury
No new lesions identified on this study
output.impression: Significant interval decrease in the size of the right parieto-occipital tumefactive lesion (4.2 cm to 2.1 cm) with complete resolution of enhancement, confirming treatment response and supporting a demyelinating diagnosis. Residual T1 black hole at the lesion center indicates some permanent tissue injury. No new lesions identified. Disease-modifying therapy should be initiated if not already started. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis, relapsing-remitting type, presenting with a large demyelinating lesion in the right parieto-occipital region causing left homonymous hemianopia

differential.key_distinguishing: Large enhancing mass with edema on initial imaging can mimic glioma; however, open ring enhancement pattern, normal MR spectroscopy choline ratios, negative CSF cytology and flow cytometry, and response to corticosteroids all argue against neoplasm

Periventricular enhancing lesion could suggest lymphoma; however, open ring rather than homogeneous enhancement, no central restricted diffusion, negative CSF flow cytometry and cytology exclude lymphoma
Tumefactive lesions can occur in NMOSD; however, AQP4 antibodies negative, additional typical MS-type periventricular lesions present, and short-segment spinal cord lesion all favor MS
MOGAD can present with tumefactive demyelination; however, MOG antibodies are negative

Positive ANA and family history of SLE raise consideration; however, anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith antibodies negative, normal complement levels, and CSF profile more consistent with demyelination than lupus cerebritis
Ring enhancement can mimic abscess; however, no central restricted diffusion on DWI, no fever, no infectious risk factors, normal WBC

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed neurological history including prior neurological episodes that may represent prior demyelinating events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identifies prior episode of right face and arm numbness 15 months ago that resolved spontaneously, representing a possible prior demyelinating event

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with focus on visual field testing and fundoscopy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left homonymous hemianopia localizing to the right parieto-occipital region; no other focal deficits; absence of papilledema

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large tumefactive lesion in right parieto-occipital white matter with open ring enhancement plus additional smaller periventricular and juxtacortical lesions

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, vitamin D, TSH, and coagulation panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal; mildly elevated ESR, positive ANA 1:80 (red herring given family history), low vitamin D

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and flow cytometry
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, oligoclonal bands present, elevated IgG index, elevated myelin basic protein; negative cytology and flow cytometry excluding malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing plus lupus-specific antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) given positive ANA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All negative, excluding NMOSD, MOGAD, and CNS lupus

optimal_actions.action: Recognize the open ring enhancement pattern as characteristic of demyelination rather than neoplasm, avoiding unnecessary biopsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Open ring enhancement with opening facing the cortex is highly specific for demyelinating lesions

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to evaluate for additional demyelinating lesions and complete DIS assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment spinal cord lesion at C5-C6 representing prior subclinical event; completes DIS across three typical MS regions

optimal_actions.action: Initiate acute treatment with high-dose IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Drug interaction check confirms safety with current medications; PPI prophylaxis recommended given concurrent ibuprofen

optimal_actions.action: Apply 2017 McDonald criteria to confirm diagnosis of MS with tumefactive presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: DIS met (periventricular + juxtacortical + spinal cord); DIT met (enhancing + non-enhancing lesions, plus prior clinical episode); CSF OCBs provide additional support

optimal_actions.action: Initiate disease-modifying therapy after acute phase, discussing high-efficacy versus moderate-efficacy options given tumefactive presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Given tumefactive presentation indicating aggressive disease, high-efficacy DMT such as natalizumab or ocrelizumab may be preferred

optimal_actions.action: Arrange follow-up MRI in 3 months and neuro-ophthalmology referral for visual field monitoring

critical_actions: Recognize the open ring enhancement pattern as characteristic of demyelination rather than neoplasm — this is the key imaging discriminator that can prevent unnecessary brain biopsy
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF cytology and flow cytometry to exclude CNS lymphoma, given the mass-like appearance of the lesion
Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which can also present with tumefactive lesions
Investigate the positive ANA with lupus-specific antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) to exclude CNS lupus, given family history of SLE
Identify the prior episode of right face/arm numbness as a possible prior demyelinating event, supporting dissemination in time
Initiate high-dose IV corticosteroids for acute treatment of the tumefactive demyelinating lesion
Start disease-modifying therapy after the acute phase to prevent future relapses
contraindicated_actions: Proceeding directly to stereotactic brain biopsy without first completing CSF analysis and autoimmune workup — biopsy carries significant risk and may be unnecessary if noninvasive testing supports demyelination
Initiating anticoagulation or thrombolysis based on the initial CT interpretation suggesting ischemic stroke — the MRI pattern is clearly inconsistent with vascular territory infarction
Diagnosing CNS lupus based solely on a positive ANA without lupus-specific antibodies, as low-to-moderate titer ANA is common in the general population
Starting teriflunomide without pregnancy testing and contraception counseling in a woman of childbearing age on oral contraceptives
Treating with chronic oral corticosteroids rather than pulse IV steroids — chronic steroids are ineffective and harmful in MS
Dismissing the prior episode of facial and arm numbness as unrelated — retrospective recognition of prior demyelinating events is essential for meeting McDonald criteria
key_reasoning_points: The initial CT suggesting acute ischemia is a clinical red herring — the patient's age (29), absence of vascular risk factors, and subsequent MRI findings are inconsistent with ischemic stroke, and the MRI pattern with ring enhancement and surrounding white matter lesions points to a demyelinating etiology

The open ring enhancement pattern, where the non-enhancing portion faces the gray matter, has high specificity (>90%) for demyelinating lesions versus tumors or abscesses — this is the single most important imaging discriminator

A positive ANA at 1:80 combined with a family history of SLE is a deliberate red herring in this case — the agent must pursue lupus-specific antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) rather than prematurely diagnosing CNS lupus based on ANA alone

The additional smaller periventricular and juxtacortical white matter lesions on MRI are critical for establishing that this is not an isolated tumefactive demyelinating lesion (ADEM-like) but rather MS with tumefactive features, as they suggest prior subclinical disease activity

CSF analysis serves dual purposes: positive oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index support intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis (demyelination), while negative cytology and flow cytometry effectively exclude CNS lymphoma

The prior episode of right facial and arm numbness 15 months ago, which the patient dismissed as stress-related, represents a prior demyelinating event that establishes dissemination in time when combined with the current presentation

Tumefactive MS (lesions >2 cm with mass effect) is an uncommon but well-recognized presentation that occurs in approximately 1-2 per 1000 MS patients and warrants consideration of high-efficacy disease-modifying therapy given the aggressive phenotype

MR spectroscopy showing mildly elevated choline with reduced NAA and lactate peak is distinguishable from high-grade glioma, which typically shows markedly elevated choline (Cho/Cr >2.0) — this additional modality can help avoid unnecessary biopsy

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate — tumefactive demyelinating lesion mimicking a brain tumor on initial imaging, with a positive ANA and family history of SLE serving as red herrings. The agent must recognize the open ring enhancement pattern as characteristic of demyelination, exclude neoplasm and CNS lupus through CSF and antibody testing, and identify a prior subclinical demyelinating episode to establish MS diagnosis. The initial CT interpretation suggesting stroke adds an additional layer of diagnostic misdirection.
clinical_pearls: Open ring enhancement, where the non-enhancing portion faces the cortex (gray matter), has >90% specificity for demyelinating lesions and is the key imaging discriminator from neoplasm

Tumefactive demyelinating lesions (>2 cm) are uncommon presentations of MS that can closely mimic brain tumors; approximately two-thirds of patients with tumefactive demyelination are eventually diagnosed with RRMS

A positive ANA alone is insufficient to diagnose CNS lupus — lupus-specific antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith) must be tested, particularly when the clinical and imaging findings point to an alternative diagnosis

MR spectroscopy can help differentiate demyelination from high-grade glioma: demyelination shows mildly elevated choline (Cho/Cr ~1.2-1.5) whereas gliomas typically show markedly elevated choline (Cho/Cr >2.0)
Patients presenting with tumefactive MS should be considered for high-efficacy disease-modifying therapy given the aggressive disease phenotype
